title: Quantum tidal locking in orbiting Bose-Einstein condensates
authors: Yaoyuan Fan, Shuoyu Shi, Lang Cao, Ziyue He, Qiuxin Zhang, Dong Hu, Yu Wang, Qing Wang, Tianwei Zhou, Xiaoji Zhou
abstract: Angular momentum coupling manifests widely in diverse physical systems, underpinning the emergent properties and collective dynamics across different scales. The tidal locking, which originates from the synchronization of rotational and orbital motions, has far-reaching impacts in celestial mechanics, reflecting fundamental processes of angular momentum transfer, energy dissipation, and evolution toward dynamical equilibrium. However, its counterpart in mesoscopic quantum fluids has remained largely unexplored. Here we demonstrate the emergence of quantum tidal locking in Bose-Einstein condensates undergoing central force motion in an anharmonic potential. The condensate follows a well-defined orbital trajectory in a static trap and experiences an effective rotating potential induced by the trap anharmonicity. The sustained geometric squeezing continuously deforms the condensate and drives a self-organized synchronization process, in which the intrinsic rotation gradually locks to the orbital motion. Numerical simulations further reveal the formation of a ring-shaped vortex array over longer timescales, arising from the coherent evolution of the rotating matter wave during the locking dynamics. Our findings establish quantum tidal locking in mesoscopic systems as a robust self-organized mechanism for generating and stabilizing circulating states.
summary: This paper explores quantum tidal locking in Bose-Einstein condensates by analyzing the coupling between the condensate's internal rotation and its overall orbital motion. Through theoretical modeling and numerical simulations, the authors show that anharmonic potentials induce a self-organization process where intrinsic angular momentum locks to the orbital frequency. This mechanism is significant because it provides a clean platform for studying fundamental synchronization phenomena in quantum matter.
main_problem: The study investigates quantum tidal locking, which is the synchronization between the intrinsic rotation and the orbital motion in mesoscopic quantum fluids.
main_result: Quantum tidal locking establishes a robust, self-organized mechanism for generating and stabilizing circulating states, leading to the formation of ring-shaped vortex arrays in Bose-Einstein condensates.
method: The work combines theoretical Hamiltonian analysis (canonical transformations) with numerical simulations solving the Gross-Pitaevskii equation to model the dynamics.
model_or_system: The system is a Bose-Einstein condensate (BEC) confined by an anharmonic potential, undergoing central force motion and exhibiting orbital angular momentum. The physics is governed by coupling terms derived from the trap's anharmonicity.
key_observables: Intrinsic angular momentum accumulation ($\hat{L}_{z}''$), convergence of intrinsic to orbital angular velocity ($\omega_{i,r} 	o \omega_c$), and the observable $\alpha$ used as a proxy for synchronization.
important_parameters: The anharmonicity parameter ($ eta$) and interaction strength ($g$) significantly influence the rate and success of the locking process.
main_assumptions: Approximations include treating the center-of-mass motion classically and discarding higher-order terms in the potential energy expansion.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the comparison across scales (microscopic to macroscopic), show the convergence of angular velocities over time, and detail four stages of vortex array formation from coherent circulation to a lattice structure.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by establishing the theoretical framework via Hamiltonian analysis, demonstrating the squeezing process leading to locking, quantifying the synchronization dynamics through observables like $\alpha$, and finally predicting the macroscopic consequence: stable ring-shaped vortex arrays.
why_interesting: This work provides a quantum mechanical analog to classical celestial mechanics (tidal locking) using ultracold atoms. It demonstrates how intrinsic non-linear interactions can drive a system into a highly ordered, synchronized state without external driving fields, which is crucial for understanding emergent collective dynamics in many-body quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Comparative diagrams of angular momentum cou- pling mechanisms across physical scales. (a) Microscopic scale: Schematic of electron spin-orbit coupling. (b) Meso- scopic scale: Schematic of rotation-orbit coupling in BECs (quantum tidal locking). (c) Macroscopic scale: Schematic of rotation-orbit coupling in celestial bodies (tidal locking in the Earth-Moon system). (d) Numerical simulation of the atom cloud at different orbital cycles, where n denotes the number of orbital revolutions, demonstrating quantum effects distinct from classical tidal locking.

FIG. 3. Experimental apparatus and results for quantum tidal locking. (a) Schematic of the experimental setup. (b) Top-view in situ absorption images at different evolution time. Upon excitation by a Bragg pulse at point A, the BECs split into two packets that revolve clockwise and counterclockwise, respectively, around point B in the horizontal plane. The angle between the major axis of each wave packet and the tangent direction of its orbital trajectory is denoted as α. Images were captured every 10 ms. At 50 ms (half of the orbital period), a second Bragg pulse was applied. A portion of each atomic cloud was transferred back to the zero-momentum state and subsequently moved toward point...

FIG. 4. (a) Numerical simulation of the formation of a ring-shaped vortex array (r0 = |X| = 80µm, ω0/2π = 2 Hz, β = −0.14, and g = 0), where n = t/T denotes the number of orbital revolutions; and an illustrative diagram of a ball bearing, serving as a visual metaphor. The vector line integral of velocity field over the yellow loop is nonzero, resulting in the vortex arrays. (b) The ring-shaped vortex array at n = 36, where vortex are distributed in multiple layers. (c) Superimposed diagram of density distribution and phase distribution.
